coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent nanoparticles for reliable communication among implantable medical devices
coredata.dc:creator: Calì, Federico
Fichera, Luca
Trusso Sfrazzetto, Giuseppe
Nicotra, Giuseppe
Sfuncia, Gianfranco
Bruno, Elena
Lanzanò, Luca
Barbagallo, Ignazio
Li-Destri, Giovanni
Tuccitto, Nunzio
coredata.dc:description: Molecular Communication (MoCo) is a bio-inspired methodology enabling secure communication in “wave-denied” environments like intrabody communication among implantable medical devices. MoCo is based on the exchange, through fluids, of molecular messengers that carry the message instead of using electromagnetic waves. The transmitter emits the symbol by releasing a certain amount of information carrier into the carrier fluid traveling to the receiver, which, located downstream, waits for detection. This innovative approach enables secure communication between active implantable medical devices with no risk of eavesdropping or interference. Here a novel modulation method is devised, theoretically simulated, and experimentally demonstrated. The information is carried by fluorescent carbon nanoparticles. The modulation of the signal is obtained by exploiting the instability effect of an interfacial phenomenon, known as viscosity fingering, which occurs when two miscible liquids having different viscosity or strong density variation makes contact. This modulation is called “Interfacial Shift Keying” (ISK). The multinary-ISK version realized by combining multiple effects of interfacial difference has allowed secure communication in a testbed simulated bionic pancreas arrangement. Values of glucose variation in the body in the hours following a meal were successfully communicated by ISK modulated MoCo. Decoding of the symbol by machine learning methods has also been comprehensively reported.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Information nanoparticles
Fluorescence
Body fluids
Fluidodynamics

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors thanks the University of Catania for the funding on the framework of “Programma ricerca di Ateneo UNICT 2020–2022 linea 2” Proj. NatI4Smart. NT thanks Lorenzo Sirna for the support in the activities during the internship period.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of intrabody communication between implantable medical devices. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

False-color images resulting from the simulations; (a) concentration of information nanoparticles present in the tube at the initial simulation time (t = 0); (b) effect of flow velocity on the spatial distribution of information nanoparticles when subjected to laminar flow inside a narrow-diameter tube under zero-slip conditions at the walls; (c) spatial distribution of nanoparticles with low diffusion coefficient (3•10−12 m [2]/s) after traveling through the entire tube; (d) same simulation conditions but with diffusion coefficient 10 times higher. Details on parameters used in the simulation are reported in Table S4. The walls of the simulated tube are considered fully reflective. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Signal detected by a receiver placed at a distance of 200 mm from the release point as obtained from simulations. The detector was considered non-absorbent (i.e., inert). Colors are associated with different values of diffusion coefficient; (b) Relevant fluorescence signal at 200 mm distance acquired during MoCo experiments. Colors are associated with different viscosity values. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) SEM micrography of an aggregated cluster of nanoparticles; b) TEM micrography of isolated nanoparticles; c) AFM height image of nanoparticles deposited on single-crystal silicon; d) histogram plot of nanoparticle height and width dimension analysis; e) fluorescence spectra of the aqueous solution of nanoparticles. Colors represent different excitation wavelength values; f) excitation-emission map of nanoparticles. Colors represent different intensities; g) FT-IR spectrum of dried nanoparticles dispersed in KBr pellets; h) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the aqueous solution of nanoparticles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a-e) False-color images representing the concentration of information nanoparticles results from numerical simulations at various increasing times; f) simulated detector signal placed at a distance of 60 mm. The colors are associated with the different transport flow velocities. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

FCS signals of information nanoparticles dissolved in (a) water and (b) glycerol/water solution having 3.5 mPas viscosity; c) comparison of diffusion time between water and glycerol solution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Experimental fluorescence intensity detected by the receiver placed at a distance of 200 mm from the transmitter; colors are associated with the different (a) viscosities of transporting flow and (b) salt concentrations. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Experimental fluorescence intensity detected by the receiver placed at a distance of 200 mm from the transmitter. Colors are associated with different flow speeds. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Various replicas of the modulated signals represented on the skewness vs kurtosis plane; b) Representative random symbol sequences' signals; c) scores plot, and d) loadings plot resulting from PCA; e) root mean spare error resulting from PLS-DA in predicted and cross-validated calculus mode as indicated by the lines' color; f) comparison between the real viscosity and the viscosity predicted by the PLS-DA model. The dots represent the recalculated false incognito samples and the red X's are for the predicted values of samples not used during the learning. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) trend of the glucose signal of a healthy adult detected during 24 h starting at 7 a.m. every 10 min; b) variation of glucose compared to the temporally precedent detection relative to the first 3 h. At time 1 h the subject had a meal; c) fluorescence signal obtained during a MoCo experiment using multinary-ISK.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent nanoparticles for reliable communication among implantable medical devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Drug and Health Sciences, Section of Biochemistry, (Scienze del Farmaco e della Salute, sezione di Biochimica), University of Catania, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecular Communication (MoCo) is a bio-inspired methodology enabling secure communication in “wave-denied” environments like intrabody communication among implantable medical devices. MoCo is based on the exchange, through fluids, of molecular messengers that carry the message instead of using electromagnetic waves. The transmitter emits the symbol by releasing a certain amount of information carrier into the carrier fluid traveling to the receiver, which, located downstream, waits for detection. This innovative approach enables secure communication between active implantable medical devices with no risk of eavesdropping or interference. Here a novel modulation method is devised, theoretically simulated, and experimentally demonstrated. The information is carried by fluorescent carbon nanoparticles. The modulation of the signal is obtained by exploiting the instability effect of an interfacial phenomenon, known as viscosity fingering, which occurs when two miscible liquids having different viscosity or strong density variation makes contact. This modulation is called “Interfacial Shift Keying” (ISK). The multinary-ISK version realized by combining multiple effects of interfacial difference has allowed secure communication in a testbed simulated bionic pancreas arrangement. Values of glucose variation in the body in the hours following a meal were successfully communicated by ISK modulated MoCo. Decoding of the symbol by machine learning methods has also been comprehensively reported.

ce:section.ce:para: Environments that do not allow the use of standard wave-based wireless systems are known as ‘wave-denied’ [1]. Such environments are characterized by conditions that cause a signal loss because the channels for communication are ‘buried or embedded’. Examples of such environments are the vessels through which biological fluids travel in animals or plants (e.g., veins and arteries, xylem and phloem). Through these channels, with a biomimetic approach, future implanted biomedical devices or diagnostic-therapeutic biorobots will need to exchange information to coordinate and perform the theranostic functions for which they were designed. Nowadays, for diagnostic purposes, implanted biosensors can detect small variations in temperature that are a symptom of emerging infections [2]; they can determine the presence of specific markers of pathologies, insert intelligent probes capable of detecting abnormal biochemical activities, etc. [3] With therapeutic aims, sophisticated implantable medical devices can perform the direct and punctual release of drugs or microprobes capable of performing real localized operations with low invasiveness [4]. However, what is still missing is a secure interconnection among implanted biodevices [5]. There is no secure platform to exchange information [6] needed to turn the isolated bionic devices into a part of a connected system that communicates, working in unison, under the control of a rational central system. The problem is a global one with a high impact, just think that among the US population overall 34.2 million people of all ages—or 10.5% of the US population—had diabetes [7]. To help them a growing number (more than 300,000 units) of continuous glucose monitor devices and insulin-releasing devices have been used by diabetic patients to deliver insulin into their circulatory systems. This assembly is called the bionic pancreas and it is also referred to as a “closed-loop automated insulin delivery system” [8]. To operate it requires the interaction of three primary components wirelessly interconnected. A real-time continuous glucose monitoring sensor inserted into the subcutaneous space [9], which measures glucose concentrations in the interstitial fluid and wirelessly sends information about the detected glucose concentrations to a device via a transmitter that houses the dosing algorithm that calculates the correct amount of insulin to be delivered based on the prediction of glucose levels, with the goal of maintaining blood glucose levels within a specific target range. Finally, it wirelessly communicates instructions to an insulin [10] or glucagon [11] pump directed by the dosing algorithm, which delivers insulin via a subcutaneous cannula. These implantable devices must be reliable, secure, and safe [12]. The intrabody communication among implantable devices can occur in ‘hostile’ environments because under fraudulent attacks or eavesdropping, with significant life safety issues for patients. The availability of new secure communication methods is of strategic importance in this emerging field of application. As history has thoroughly demonstrated, from Caesar's cipher in the Roman ages to the infamous Enigma machine of the German armed forces cleverly decrypted by Polish mathematicians, having the ability to establish encrypted communication in hostile contexts can make the difference between triumphing or succumbing. Recently an innovative communication platform based on new information carriers to replace electromagnetic waves in response to these crucial challenges obtaining intrinsically secure communication has been devised. This new branch of communication is called ‘Molecular Communication’ (MoCo) [13]. The approach is bio-inspired and openly traces the mechanisms that nature, over millennia of evolution, has refined to pass on information to offspring or regulate the biochemical cycles at the basis of the proper functioning of functional organs. The ingenious criteria for encoding the genetic heritage, the sophisticated and efficient mechanism for translating this information leading to protein synthesis, or the regulation of energy metabolic cycles assigned to hormones, are some paradigmatic examples of the use of molecular systems as ‘information carriers’. Three blocks ideally represent the simplest arrangement of platforms for MoCo [14]: transmitter, channel, receiver (as schematized in Fig. 1 ). The transmitter emits the symbol by releasing a certain amount of information carrier into the carrier fluid in the shortest possible time (ideally instantaneously). This compact stream will travel, transported by the fluid flowing in the channel, to the receiver, which, located downstream, waits for detection. This communication is intrinsically secure with no risk of eavesdropping or interference because not being based on electromagnetic radiations is not remotely interceptable.

To enable the effective and safe exchange of information between the transmitter and the receiver, an information carrier requires to be tolerated by the environment in which the transmitter emits them, and secondly, they must possess a property allowing an agile detection by the receiver by selectively revealing of them concerning other components flowing in the transporting channel [15]. Our strategy is based on the employment of nanoparticles as information carriers. We usually call them ‘information nanoparticles’. In particular, we use Carbon Quantum Dots (CD) [42]. CD are obtained from organic precursors, which, upon decomposition, evolve towards the formation of nanoparticles mainly based on carbon, plus certain heteroatoms, such as O, N, or S, depending on the synthesis strategy pursued [16–19]. We have also prepared them from waste products from the agri-food chain to pursue a circular economy criterion [43]. They are water-soluble and typically generally assessed as biocompatible [21,22], and therefore suitable for communication through biological fluids. Another unique feature is their high fluorescence efficiency in the visible light spectrum (between 400 and 650 nm) when excited in the ultraviolet region (below 380 nm) [23]. Similar to what can be done with so-called inorganic quantum dots, the fluorescence of nanoparticles can also be modulated synthetically at will by incorporating heteroatoms or changing their size [16–19]. It is precisely this fluorescence that we exploit to make them selectively detectable by the receiver, and which has recently enabled us to create the first working prototype of an IoT network based entirely on information nanoparticles made up of fluorescent carbon quantum dots [24]. Here, we show that such fluorescent nanoparticles enable secure communication among implanted biodevices.

In standard communication via electromagnetic waves, symbols are encoded by modulating the properties of the carrier signal. Carrier waves can be described mathematically by sine waves as:
(1)
s
( t )
=
A w
sin
( 2 π f t + φ )
where
A w
is the amplitude of the wave,
f
is the frequency of the wave (Hz), and
φ
is the phase of the wave (rad). The information is encoded on a sine wave using any of three properties: Amplitude-Shift Keying (ASK), Frequency-Shift Keying (FSK), and Phase-Shift Keying (PSK). There is no limit to using a single modulation scheme, and hybrid combinations can be used. In MoCo, information can be encoded according to the transmitter's number of nanoparticles (or concentration). This modulation is called “Concentration Shift Keying” (CSK) [25]. In this way, it is possible to transfer information in binary code, encoding the signal 1-bit by releasing information nanoparticles with a concentration above a predetermined threshold and 0-bit if lower. If C is the concentration of information nanoparticles detected by the receiver at time t, the logic behind the decoding is described as follows:
(2)
C
( t )
=
{
<
C
t h r e s h o l d
for bit 0
>
C
t h r e s h o l d
for bit 1

To implement this modulation, it is necessary to know how to set the value of the threshold concentration Cthreshold. This value can be defined as the minimum limit of detection (LOD) of the detector placed at the receiver. In practice, a multiplicative factor is applied, which in our experience is typically 10 times the LOD. Once this value has been defined, it is possible to calculate the initial concentration of “information particles” that the transmitter must release when communicating bit 1. The value of Cbit-1 will depend on the dilution factor that the stream of information particles undergoes during its journey. The equation governing this process is the advection-diffusion-reaction:
(3)
∂ C
∂ t
=
∇ ⋅
(
D
I P
∇ C
)
− ∇ ⋅
(
v C
)
+ Ω
where
D
I P
is the diffusion coefficient of the information particles,
Ω
expresses the kinetic equations of the chemical reactions in which they are involved (if applicable), and
v
is the term representing the velocity field along the transport channel. In the simplest, one-dimensional case, where the receiver is placed at a distance L from the transmitter, the shape of the signal is Gaussian, and the value to be emitted can be derived [26,27] as:
(4)
C
b i t _ 1
=
C
t h r e s h o l d
( 4 π D t )
− 1 / 2
e
−
( L − v t )
2
4 D t

Multinary modulation by defining multiple thresholds has also been proposed [28]. Instead of a single threshold, the symbol can be adapted to represent bits using 2b different values with 2b - 1 threshold levels. Another modulation method is based on the reactivity of information nanoparticles. It is called “Reaction Shift Keying” (RSK) [29], and it exploits chemical reactions to encode the symbol, e.g., 0-bit when unreacted and 1-bit when reacted. To describe the process theoretically, it is necessary to include the reaction kinetics in Eq. (3) and find numerical solutions, depending on the nature of the transport channel. Reactivity is also involved in stabilizing the signal in the case of MoCo occurring with variable velocity drift [30]. In addition, modulation can be performed by regulating some physicochemical features of the information carrier. The first proposed modulation is based on the chemical difference of molecules, used as messengers that exceed a certain threshold when detected by the receiver. It has been called “Molecular Shift Keying” (MoSK) [31]. This method can also increase the bandwidth similarly to CSK by obtaining a multinary encoding, going from 2-bit Binary MoSK to 4-bit Quadruple MoSK, and so on.

Along with these, many other modulations have been theorized; however, along the road from the conception of the modulation to its practical implementation, it is soon discovered that the main limiting factor of MoCo is undoubtedly the low bandwidth due to the restrictions imposed by the transport speed of the information nanoparticles, which is directly dependent on the fluid-dynamic characteristics of the transporting medium. The information nanoparticles cannot travel faster than transporting fluid, with stochastic diffusion being a very slow process. A possible strategy for solving this problem lies in increasing the density of information encoded within the single emission of information nanoparticles. Once again, this is a biomimetic strategy. Nature does not communicate by the methods described above, i.e., releasing sequences of particles representative of bit sequences. Evolved organisms enclose the entire message to be transmitted in a single release of the messenger in the biological transport fluid. This implies that the transfer of information does not involve the emission of successive, appropriately modulated signals but rather the release of a specific molecule, such as a hormone, which carries all the information necessary to activate a specific biochemical response. We have recently shown that it is possible to do this by releasing different information particles [32]. By associating each of them with a different message, we have shown that it is even possible to set up a simple Internet of Things system based on molecular communication [24]. This approach, although practical, has very strong limitations related to the need to change the nanoparticle to encode the message. This imposed a great deal of technical effort at the receiver development to be able to correctly detect the different information particles. Our research group has worked firmly to overcome these limitations. This is the last challenge tackled in this work. To devise a modulation that allows us to vary the shape of the signal detected by the receiver by assigning a precise message to each shape by using a single type of information particle. The modulation we present here is extremely simple to implement since it can be achieved by using a single type of “information particle” but adding a substance (also tolerated by the environment in which it travels) that locally alters a physical property of the carrier fluid, namely viscosity.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were chosen as information particles. The CQDs used in this work were prepared from citric acid (Merck, Italy), using the pyrolysis method. A quantity of about 10 g of citric acid is weighed and placed inside a beaker on a heating plate at a temperature between 200 and 250 °C. This pyrolysis process produces a dark brown caramel after 10–15 min. This is cooled and then a 0.2 M NaOH solution (Merck, Italy) is added to it until a neutral pH is reached. The resulting suspension is subjected to membrane dialysis (Membra-Cel MC18 with a molecular weight cut-off of 14,000 Da) to remove all water-soluble pyrolysis residues and by-products. Dialysis is continued, periodically changing the water, until non-fluorescent dialysis water is obtained (typically after 2 days). Finally, a cryo-centrifugation step is carried out for 2 h at 10000 rpm and 3 °C. The supernatant is recovered and subjected to subsequent characterization and MoCo experiments. The typical concentration of the ‘as prepared’ solution is around 2 mg/ml. NMR-DOSY information was gathered using a Varian UNITY Inova 500 MHz spectrometer (1H at 499.88 MHz, 13C NMR at 125.7 MHz) equipped with a pulse-field gradient module (Z-axis) and a tuneable 5 mm Varian inverse detection probe (ID-PFG). Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was performed by Gemini 152 field emission SEM Supra 25 (Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). SEM images were obtained in the Inlens mode. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a Jeol ARM200F equipped with a cold-field emission gun having a 0.27 eV energy spread. TEM analysis was conducted using an accelerating voltage of 200 KV, in conventional parallel beam configuration (C-TEM) and low electron dose conditions. TEM micrographs were recorded using a Gatan UltraScan 1000XP (2k × 2k) charge-coupled device camera. TEM specimen was prepared by drop-casting few microliters of CQDs dispersion on a TEM grid supporting an ultrathin (<3 nm) amorphous carbon film. FT-IR was performed by disperding CD in KBr pellets with Spectrum Two (Perkin lmer). Cytotoxicity tests were performed by Bromide 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium (MTT) Assay. Human non-tumorigenic lung epithelial cell line (BEAS-2B) was treated for 24 h in 96-well plates with different concentrations of Fluorescent nanoparticle, respectively 5 mg/ml, 2.5 mg/ml, 0.5 mg/ml, 250 μg/ml, 125 μg/ml, 50 μg/ml and 5 μg/ml. Successively, the medium was replaced by a solution containing bromide 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium (MTT) and incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. Finally, every plate well was added with 100 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and then read by a spectrophotometer at λ = 570 nm (PMID 31261958).

Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed by means of Cary 60 (Agilent). Fluorescence Correlation Spectroscopy (FCS) was performed on a Leica SP8 confocal microscope (Leica Microsystems, Wetzlar, Germany) equipped with a 1.4 NA 63 × oil immersion objective (HCPL APO CS2). The excitation wavelength was set to 405 nm and the emission was detected in the band 412–565 nm via a hybrid detector operated in photon counting mode. The solutions containing the CD (normally diluted 1/500 from the ‘as prepared’) were placed inside eightwell chamber plates (glass bottom, thickness 170 ± 5 μm) (ibidi, Planegg, Germany). The focal spot was placed inside the solution, at about 2 μm above the glass. Data (fluorescence intensity vs time) were acquired in slow scanning mode [33], by setting the scanner speed at the minimum value allowed by the acquisition software (v = 154 μm/s). The temporal resolution was set to 3.04 μs and the duration of a measurement was about 100 s. The data were exported and processed in Matlab (Mathworks) to calculate the intensity autocorrelation function (ACF), defined as:
G
( τ )
=
I
( t )
I
(
t + τ
)
I
( t )
2
− 1
where I(t) is the intensity at time t. The ACF were fitted to a model of Brownian diffusion through a Gaussian detection volume:
G
( τ )
= G
( 0 )
1
1 +
τ
τ D
S
( τ )
where G(0) is the amplitude of the ACF and τ
D is the diffusion time. τ
D represents the average transit time of the nanoparticles through the focal spot, τ
D = w [2]/4D, where D is the diffusion coefficient and w is 1/e2 size of the focal spot in the lateral direction. The term S(τ) contains the correlations due to the motion of the scanner and is given by S(τ) = exp[-(v [2]τ [2]/w2)/(1+τ/τD)], where v is the speed of the scanner.

The testbed prototype platform mimicking a bionic pancreas used for testing the molecular communication consists of the following parts: an infusion pump (IVAC P3000), a six-way valve for releasing the nanoparticles (Reodyne, IDEX Health & Science), a PTFE tube ID 1/32″ OD 1/16″, a fluorescence detector (Shimadzu, rf535) operating at 365 nm excitation and 450 nm emission. Carrier solutions were prepared by mixing water (ultrapure filtered, Millipore Merck, Italy) and glycerol (Merck, Italy) until the desired viscosity was achieved. The viscosity was measured with a viscometer tube (Cannon-Fenske, ASTM D446).

Numerical resolution of differential equations has been performed by the authors without using commercial software. Python programming language was used with the benefit of the numerical algebra library named NUMPY and the finite volume partial differential equations solver named FiPy [34]. Concentration maps resulting from the simulations were prepared using the Matplotlib library. Scripts are available from the authors upon request.

To know the fate of the information nanoparticles from the transmitter to the receiver it is necessary to solve Eq. (3), albeit numerically. In the case under investigation, the reactivity is omitted and the channel is represented by a hypothetical cylinder vessel of micrometric diameter (of radius r) and length (L) of the order of tens of centimeters in which the laminar regime is in force. The fluid, having viscosity η, is conducted through the straight and rigid channel, imposing a pressure difference (ΔP) between the two ends. Under these conditions, Poiseuille's law returns us the velocity profile, i.e. the velocities v(r) at different radial positions between the center (r = 0) and the wall (r = R), inside a capillary or a cylindrical channel, is given by:
(6)
v
( r )
=
Δ P
4 η L
(
R 2
−
r 2
)

Similar considerations can be made for channels that are more complex in geometry and in the case of non-perfectly laminar flows such as human blood. In this case, it will be necessary to solve the differential equations governing the motion to find the velocity field. From eq. (6) it is clear that the Poiseuille velocity profile for ducts with very small R is parabolic, with the maximum velocity at the center of the duct. This will imply that the concentration profile of the information nanoparticles at the detector, and consequently the shape of the symbol signal received by the receiver, resulting from the solution of eq. (3) will not always be of Gaussian type. As far as the diffusion coefficient is concerned, the Stokes-Einstein equation is a good approximation for solving Eq. (3):
(7)
D
I P
=
k B
T
6 π η
r
I N
where
k B
is the Boltzmann constant,
T
is the temperature,
r
I N
is the hydrodynamic radius of the information nanoparticles. Fig. 2 shows simulation results for a microfluidic channel having
R = 0.1
mm and L
= R ⋅ 1000
. Assuming that the value of the diffusion coefficient of the information nanoparticles is isotropic, it is possible to break it down into two contributions: axial and transverse (or radial) to the flow. Since there is advection along the flow direction, the axial contribution of diffusion can be considered negligible. Transversely, on the other hand, there is no movement of the carrier, so the only contribution to the movement of the information nanoparticles is attributable to diffusion stimulated by the transverse concentration gradient, if any.

At the beginning of the process, when the plug of information nanoparticles has just been released and is extremely compact, no transverse gradient is present (Fig. 2a). As it continues along its path due to the velocity flow given by Eq. (6), the compact plug will assume a shape that we can define as “arrow-shaped” (Fig. 2b) generating soon a transverse concentration gradient between the area where the carrier is pure and the two interfaces with the regions of high particle concentration, both the front (arrow head) and the back (arrow tails). If the diffusion coefficient is relatively low and the speed and length of the channel are such that the receiver can be reached quickly, then the transmitted signal will have a strongly asymmetrical shape. The receiver will detect in the first phase a concentration of information nanoparticles relative to the central part of the tube characterized by the highest transport speed. This will lead to the formation of an initial peak followed by a long tail relating to the arrival at the destination of the nanoparticles that have traveled in the areas closest to the walls, and therefore slower. The concentration profile of the nanoparticles is characterized by a faster part (front) in which the concentration is greater at the center of the channel than at the walls, and a later part (back) in which the concentration is greater at the walls of the channel than at the center. Relative to this, the total effect of transverse diffusion is to slow down the anterior interface and speed up the posterior interface, thus keeping the concentration distribution more symmetrical along the axial direction (Fig. .2c–d). This phenomenon is known as Taylor effect [35]. An increase in the diffusion coefficient or a decrease in the flow velocity affects the dispersion of the substance in a microfluidic channel, in the same way, i.e. they increase the Taylor effect.

Fig. 3 a shows the signal detected by the receiver as the diffusion coefficient changes under the same conditions of velocity and channel size. The effect on the shape of the received signal is very clear. One could, therefore, exploit this variation to modulate the signal by assigning a different symbol to each shape. To validate these theoretical results, we synthesized information nanoparticles.

Fig. 4 a report the results related to the characterization of the nanoparticles employing SEM showing the formation of micrometer-sized aggregates due to stacking aggregation. This phenomenon is known in the literature and is typical of concentrated solutions [36]. This behavior is associated with the fluorescence's self-quenching revealed at concentrations above 0.1 mg/ml (see Fig. S1). By diluting the solution, SEM images (see Fig. S2) show isolated particles, but the small size of the nanoparticles makes the quality of the results difficult. Therefore, they were analyzed by TEM (Fig. 4b) and AFM (Fig. 4c). Fig. 4d reports the AFM histogram analysis of the height and width. This morphological characterization reveals that the pyrolysis synthesis of Carbon Dots results in nanoparticles having a diameter ranging from around 2–3 nm up to about 20 × 20 times larger. However, it is challenging to evaluate the effect of tip convolution with these dimensions. Therefore, TEM microscopy is more appropriate. TEM analysis showed single well-dispersed CQDs with diameters ranging from 6 to 22 nm. Fig. 4e–f reports the fluorescence spectrum obtained with a different excitation wavelength and the relative excitation-emission map, respectively. The strongest emission peak is centered at 460 nm upon excitation at 370 nm. The FT-IR spectrum, reported in Fig. 4g, shows the characteristic peaks for the stretching vibration of CC at 1576 cm−1, CO at 1704 cm−1, C–H at 2930 cm−1, and O–H at 3406 cm−1. One can observe also a characteristic peak for the bending vibration of O–H at 1405 cm−1. UV–Vis spectrum gives us the typical absorption band centered at 335 nm, indicating the n-π∗ transition caused by surface functional groups, such as CO. Cytotoxicity assays showed that the CD solutions up t0 5 mg/ml concentration did not exhibit cytotoxic activity against human cells (Fig. S4).

Comprehensive results show that the nanoparticles synthesized by us are suitable candidates as information nanoparticles for molecular communication under conditions of variable diffusion coefficient. From an experimental point of view, to vary the diffusion coefficient, it is necessary to act, as shown by eq. (7), on the viscosity of the flow. Fig. 3b shows the profile of the fluorescence signals detected by the receiver as the viscosity of the fluid changes when a single plug of information nanoparticles is released by the transmitter. The variation of the signal shape is in line with the theoretical predictions (Fig. 3a) although less recognizable, compared to the simulation, because experimentally the diffusion coefficient was not significantly changed. To reach this condition, it would be necessary to have large differences in viscosity. However, this will cause limiting effects on the operating conditions, since the pressures necessary to guarantee constant flow velocity even at high viscosities become prohibitive and the solubility of the particles is not always guaranteed by varying this fluid property so much. However, the results obtained allow us to confirm the theoretical ones. Signal modulation by varying the viscosity affecting the diffusion coefficient of information nanoparticles has been conceptualized, described theoretically, and experimentally verified for the first time. We call this modulation “Viscosity Shift Keying” (VSK). This is a modulation unique to MoCo that has no equivalence in classical electromagnetic wave communication. Fig. S5 shows the communication of a message (“HELLO WORLD”) by using VSK. However, the variation of the signal shape induced by different Ds is hardly perceptible. Although features could be found to discriminate the shapes of the different signals associated with the various symbols (peak height, signal width, skewness, kurtosis, etc.) the discrimination is complex and would certainly lead to poor communication in terms of bit error rate. Although practicable in this form, VSK modulation is difficult to apply “in operando” since it can be cumbersome to vary the viscosity of the whole flow of the channel in which information particles are transported. However, since VSK modulation is physically based on the ability to control the asymmetry of the signal at the detector, it will be sufficient to modify only the viscosity of the injected particles-plug, exploiting the instabilities of the interfaces between miscible liquids having different chemical-physical properties, such as the well-known Rayleigh-Taylor instability [37]. From a theoretical point of view, this implies that the D
IP of the particles will depend on the position they are in since the fluid has viscosity η that varies in space and time. See the effect of η on D
IP in eq. (7). Assuming that two miscible liquids with significantly different viscosities (up to 7.5 times) are used, the diffusion coefficient D
IP of the information nanoparticles will be given, in good approximation, by the Arrhenius equation, namely: the logarithm of the viscosity of the mixture is the sum of the logarithms of the viscosities of the individual components weighted by their relative amounts in the mixture:
(8)
ln
D
I P
( x , y , z , t )
=
χ
⋅ ln
D
h i g h t
+
( 1 − χ )
⋅ ln
D
l o w
where D
hight and D
low are the particle diffusion coefficients in the two pure fluids at lower and higher viscosities, respectively. Both are calculated from eq. (7). χ is the mole fraction of the most viscous fluid calculated at the point in space and time considered:
(9)
∂ χ
∂ t
=
∇ ⋅
(
D
m i x
∇ χ
)
− ∇ ⋅
(
v χ
)
where D
mix is the diffusion coefficient derived from the mix of the two liquids calculated according to the mixture-averaged diffusion approximation, namely: the diffusion is proportional to a single diffusion coefficient. The coefficient describes the diffusion of species relative to the remaining mixture and is referred to as the mixture-averaged diffusion coefficient. As far as the velocity field is concerned, in conditions where two liquids of different viscosity or density are interfacing, it is known that instability phenomena occur causing the formation of the so-called viscosity fingering [38]. Viscous fingering is a flow instability phenomenon that results in the destabilization of the interface between two fluids of differing viscosities. The destabilized interface results in a complex mixture of the two fluids in a pattern that resembles fingers. The conditions that enhance this type of flow instability are typically found in chromatographic separation systems. There are several models for the treatment of these peculiar interphase phenomena. We use here a simplified model proposed by Rousseaux and co-authors as it appears a good approximation of the phenomenon we want to investigate [39]. Basically, the instability can be taken into account considering a variation ζ of the velocity field directly proportional to the spatial concentration gradient of the two miscible liquids. At each instant t the velocity is given by:
(10)
v
I N S T
= v + k
[ v ξ ∇ χ
(
η
I P
−
η
c a r r i e r
)
]
where ξ is a random number between o and 1. k is a proportionality constant that expresses the amount of instability induced at the interphase and depends on the chemical and physical properties of the two liquids that meet. It is a positive number.
η
I P
and
η
c a r r i e r
are the viscosities of the solution containing the information nanoparticles and of the carrier, respectively. Eq. (10) ensures that
v
I N S T
is negligible when velocity is very low, e.g. close to the pipe wall. In areas where the mole fraction is constant (
∇ χ = 0
), the instability will be vanishing and the velocity field is not affected. In interface zones, where there is a mole fraction gradient (
∇ χ ≠ 0
), the instability will cause the fingering effect.

Fig. 5 a–e shows the simulation results obtained by including Eq. (10) in Eq. (9) and solving it numerically in the case of
η
I P
<
η
c a r r i e r
. The effect of the instability causes the formation of viscosity fingering which will certainly influence the distribution of the information nanoparticles along the journey towards the receivers. Having calculated the mole fraction, it is possible to derive D
IP
(x, y, z, t) to be used to simulate the transport of the information nanoparticles from the receiver to the transmitter in this new fluid dynamic condition in which the viscosity η is not constant, using the equation
(11)
∂
C
I P
∂ t
=
∇ ⋅
(
D
I P
∇
C
I P
)
− ∇ ⋅
(
v
I N S T
C
I P
)

Fig. 5f shows the simulated detected signal by the receiver when released information nanoparticles are dissolved in a liquid of low viscosity (modulating) and transported by fluid of high viscosity (carrier). Detected signals by the detector as a function of the flow velocity reveal the appearance of peaks in the upslope area of the signal which is not present with the condition of equiviscosity (see Fig. 3). All these results allow us to state that our idea of modulating the signal without changing the type of information nanoparticles but locally varying the viscosity of the liquid in which they are dissolved while keeping the carrier fluid unchanged is supported by the in-silico study. We call this new modulation “Interfacial Shift Keying” (ISK). Consequently, we undertook an extensive experimental campaign to verify its feasibility in-operando. We performed MoCo experiments between a transmitter emitting fluorescent information nanoparticles in which the microfluidic channel has a viscosity of 3.5 mPa
⋅
s. It should be noted that 3.5 mPa
⋅
s is close to the viscosity of human blood. The transmitter operated the ISK by emitting fluorescent nanoparticles dissolved in water/glycerol solutions having viscosity varying between 1 mPa
⋅
s and 7.5 mPa
⋅
s to induce the (local) variation of diffusion coefficient. To this end, we experimentally verified that the change in viscosity correlated with the change in D
IP . That is, the ratio of D
IP in glycerol solution to D
IP in water was equal to the viscosity ratios, approximately 3.5 times.

We measured the ratio D
IP in glycerol to D
IP in water by Fluorescence Correlation Spectroscopy (FCS) (Fig. 6 ). FCS is based on the analysis of fluorescence intensity fluctuations generated by diffusion of the fluorescent particles through a small detection volume [33,40]. For the CD in water, we measured a diffusion time τ
D (water) = 13 ± 3 μs (mean ± s.d., n = 4) whereas for the CD in glycerol solution having 3.5 mPas viscosity, we measured a diffusion time τ
D (glycerol solution) = 35 ± 4 μs (mean ± s.d., n = 4). The ratio of the diffusion coefficients is given by the inverse of the ratio of the diffusion times measured by FCS in the two solvents, D
IP (water)/D
IP (glycerol solution) = τ
D (glycerol solution)/τ
D (water) = 2.7 ± 0.7. The results confirm that Eq. (8) is a good approximation in the case under investigation.

The signals shown in Fig. 7 a were obtained by keeping the carrier fluid at viscosity 3.5 mPa
⋅
s (carrier) in all experiments and changing the viscosity of the injected solution (modulating) containing the fluorescent nanoparticles. The results confirm what was obtained with the simulations. We note the features due to the instability of the interphase in the “upslope” part of the signal for the first two cases at lower local viscosity, respectively with a nanoparticles suspension viscosity of 1 mPa
⋅
s and 2.5 mPa
⋅
s. The signals corresponding to an higher viscosity of the injected solution than that of the carrier, i.e. 5 mPa
⋅
s and 7.5 mPa
⋅
s, are characterized by a signal shape with a long tail as predicted by the relative simulation model. At the highest viscosity, “features” also appear in the latter part of the peak due to viscosity fingering in the tail of the nanoparticle stream traveling towards the receiver. We repeated the same measurements numerous times for each viscosity, keeping the carrier fluid at 3.5 mPa
⋅
s viscosity. The stability of the signal shape obtained between replicates (please see Fig. S3) verifies the reproducibility of our experimental setup and validates the new “ISK” modulation. A diligent criticism of our interpretation of the physical phenomenon underlying the “ISK” modulation might raise the objection: could the instability phenomenon also occur under conditions of density difference even with the same kinematic viscosity? Following a rigorous scientific approach, we designed experiments to test this hypothesis. In particular, we have prepared solutions that are equimolar to those used in the experiments in Fig. 7a, but using a salt (namely sodium chloride) as the solute. In this way, we have obtained a condition in which there is a density gradient, but no change in the viscosity of the solutions since it does not vary significantly by the addition of the salt. Table S1 shows the viscosities and densities of the various salt solutions (T = 25 °C). Furthermore, we have verified by DOSY-NMR that the diffusion coefficient of the nanoparticles is not strongly influenced by the presence of sodium chloride in solution either (D = 3.7 × 10−10 m [2]/sec). The signals received using sodium chloride solutions are shown in Fig. 7b compared to those obtained using glycerol confirm that the signal modulation characterist ics are due to physical phenomena well described by our theoretical model, experimentally reproducible and far from a random occurrence. Comparable considerations can be made when comparing the effect of the velocity of the carrier flow on the signal shape. See the similarity of the experimental signals shown in Fig. 8 with the theoretical ones shown in Fig. 5f. The correspondence is undoubted. The source of instability is undoubtedly dependent on the instrumental conditions. In particular, our experiments show that it is most likely arising from the plug injection section of information nanoparticles. It is important to note, however, that for the same experimental setup the shape of the signal features is extremely reproducible and repeatable.

Having defined and verified the new ISK modulation by the transmitter the query now turns to the receiver of the message. We show here how to provide the receiver the ability to recognize symbols encoded using the ISK. From the analysis of the symmetry (or asymmetry) of the differently modulated signal through the “ISK”, it is possible to obtain basic descriptors to be used for the recognition of the symbol assigned to the individual signal form at the detector. Fig. 9 a shows the various replicas of the modulated signals represented on the skewness vs kurtosis plane. An acceptable classification by solution viscosity of injected nanoparticles is obtained only if they are very different from each other. A certain overlap of some classes is noted and the two parameters appear strongly correlated. Even though this is a very simple method to implement, this removes the possibility of using Skewness and Kurtosis as descriptors for “fine” recognition of similar signals. Other descriptors of equal simplicity of implementation, such as the width of the signal at half-height, the position of the maximum, etc., proved equally useless. We, therefore, decided to approach the problem from a multivariate point of view by applying Principal Component Analysis (PCA). We constructed a matrix in which the objects are represented by the various replicas of the modulated signals and the intensity values acquired by the detector, at various times, represent the variables. We obtained a matrix consisting of 50 rows (objects) by 1000 columns (variables).

Fig. 9c–f shows the results of the PCA. Section 9c shows the scores-plot, where can be seen a very good clustering of the points associated with different symbols in the plane of the first two principal components, which sums up to about 90% of the explained variance. Section 9d reports the loadings-plot from which one can derive which are the “features” of the modulated signals that best characterize the various symbols. The characteristic intensity points that allow the symbols to be discriminated range from the 200th to the 700th time-step (s). In other words, in this interval each signal is described by distinct intensity values that together give a signal shape that changes from symbol to symbol, depending on the viscosity. Intensity points obtained before the 200th and after the 700th accumulate on the Loadings Plot close to zero, indicating that they do not affect the principal components and are therefore useless in discriminating between symbols. This makes it possible to define the start and end zone of the symbol transmission very precisely. These points describe the initial noise before the substance arrives at the detector and part of the final tail of each signal, which is extremely similar for all viscosities. This has practical implications both for the definition of the threshold value beyond which the detected signal is considered relevant and for defining the width of the individual symbol objectively. We have selected time-steps significant as they fall in the loadings plot outside a circle of radius 0.01. In the light of these encouraging results, having defined how to size the single symbol to be transmitted (in terms of duration and intensity) we have processed dozens of combinations of symbols emitted in random order. For recognition, we applied a data processing method more suitable than PCA for classification, namely PLS-DA. Fig. 9e shows that the “best performance fitting” of the dataset in the “cross-validated” mode is obtained by using 4 principal components for the PLS-DA model. Fig. 9f shows the correlation between the viscosity (i.e. the symbol) used for the modulation and that predicted by the model. The result is satisfactory. Note how three different random symbol sequences (shown in Fig. 9b) that were not included in the dataset for model fitting were correctly recognized. Moreover, two values of viscosity (namely: 2 and 4 mPas) were properly detected even if never included in the training set. This undoubtedly confirms the applicability of the “ISK” modulation for multi-symbol transmission through microfluidic channels. To increase the number of transmittable symbols, we combined several successive close releases. Thus, it will be possible to increase the structuring of the signal shape by releasing two solutions of information nanoparticles of different viscosities at close times in such a way as to cause the two plugs to overlap on their journey to the receiver. The implementation of this modulation strategy is easy to apply by using an automated multiways valve for symbol releasing (see SCHEME-1SI). 32 differently modulated signals are obtained by combining the 5 viscosities. We used them in our experiments resulting in a significant increase in the communication bandwidth to quickly transfer complex information by multinary-ISK. By combining any selective detection capabilities of different nanoparticles by the detector, the available symbol set could be further extended. For example, by combining nanoparticles with different fluorescence emissions with a multispectral detector, as we recently demonstrated [32], the number of symbols would grow exponentially.

When applying MoCo inside the body, either for the communication of single information or for the connection and integration of multiple intra-body devices, it can be defined as “intrinsically secure” since it cannot be intercepted. The information nanoparticles cannot be revealed remotely, they are not magnetic, or metal based, they do not produce any electromagnetic field detectable by an external observer. The only way to capture the message requires withdrawing the carrier liquid from the vessel where it contains the information nanoparticles or install -inline- another fluorescence detector. But even in this case, the decoding of the message would be very complex (although feasible in principle) because the shape of the signal depends on the path that the information nanoparticles have done. Fig. S5 reports a simulated signal detected by a receiver placed at 3 different distances with flow traveling at 1 μl/s. Results reveals that an external observer trying to eavesdrop the signal at a different distance from the receiver (before or after) would detect a different signal shape making it very complicated to read the symbols correctly.

To demonstrate the actual relevance of our studies, we applied multinary-ISK modulation in the medical field for the molecular communication between two active implantable medical devices: device 1 is a continuous glucose monitoring having the function of monitoring the glycaemic level in the blood and device 2 being an implantable microinfusor having the function of releasing the treatment (insulin or glucagon) in case of hyper- or hypo-glycaemic peaks, so making a prototypal communication testbed mimicking a bionic pancreas [41]. Fig. 10 a shows the typical pattern of blood glucose concentration during 24 h, noting the 3 peaks due to daily meals. The glucose concentration is maintained under control by the resulting release of insulin or glucagon by the healthy body activities.

The experimental setup involves the transferring of the value of the change in glucose level detected by device 1 to be communicated to device 2 at regular time intervals (every 10 min) rounded to the nearest integer, whenever it exceeds the threshold value of ±1 mg/dl. Fig. 10b shows the change in glucose concentration relative to the breakfast peak (duration 3 h) acquired every 10 min that requires communication from devices 1 to devices 2. Table S2 shows the correlation between the value the transmitter intends to communicate and the next pair of subsequent bits to release. Fig. 10C shows signals related to each symbol received by devices 2. Please note the initial delay due to the transport of information nanoparticles from transmitter to receiver. A properly trained receiver (as shown above) would correctly recognize the symbol allowing the triggering at device 2 to release the treatment. Although the two medical devices were simulated (not actually present in our experiment) and the glucose control is due to natural molecular communication in the body of the healthy subject, our results undoubtedly demonstrate that the appropriate delivery of fluorescent nanoparticle solutions dissolved in solutions of varying viscosity enables secure molecular communication in wave-denied environments. Although in MoCo the communication speed is limited by the carrier drift, ISK allowed as to strongly increase the bandwidth. Fig. S6 report the communication of a single value (namely: +3) transferred by “conventional” molecular communication based on the binary CSK converting the ASCII message to binary code (namely: 00101011 00110011 00001010). The “conventional” communication will take around 4 h to transfer the message. Comparing with 15 min need by ISK modulation, we derived an increasing of the channel capacity around 15 times bigger.

Finally, ISK represent a significant advancement in the field of ON-OFF concentration-based signal modulation with respect to CSK and RSK, CSK modulation represents digital data as the presence or absence (or under threshold) of the information particles. RSK represents digital data as the reacted or non-reacted information particles. In the ISK the symbol is represented by means of the shape of the fluorescence signal detected by the receiver when the information nanoparticles travel in the channel experiencing interfaces instabilities induced by modulation the concentration of a miscible liquids having different viscosity (or density) with respect to the carriers (glycerol and water in this case). This enables a significant increase of the bandwidth and, as a consequence, a great reduction of the time required to communicate information.

We showed the implementation of a new communication methodology enabling secure communication among active medical devices. The strategy we have devised and demonstrated to work, is based on the abolition of electromagnetic radiation usage to communicate. The environment in which the implanted devices are located is “wave-denied” and the information that the devices exchange is highly sensitive. Interception of such data could cause health damage by affecting the efficiency of treatment. The hacking of communications could even be lethal by causing overdoses. The implementation of MoCo in this context solves the problem at its root because it excludes remote access, relegating it to the intra-body area while maintaining compatibility. However, the typical limitation of molecular communication is mainly represented by the slowness of the transport medium. We have shown that using the effect of viscosity fingering it is possible to modulate the shape of the signal in such a way as to associate the desired symbol to every single release. We have designed, simulated, and experimentally verified the multinary-ISK modulation. It proved to be extremely effective in the case study that we exploited to test its capabilities. The modulation of the signal shape could be, if necessary, even more, sophisticated by exploiting the timing between successive signals by partially overlapping them, increasing the combinations of viscosity/density, or mixing information nanoparticles having different fluorescence. Our studies show that, in this perspective, the MoCo can become a methodology ready for implementation on an application scale.

Federico Calì: performed the synthesis and part of the characterization of CD and implemented MoCo exepriments, wrote the draft article; created the visualization/data presentation. Luca Fichera: implemented the numerical simulation related to the simulation of MoCo exepriments; wrote the software; wrote the draft article. Giuseppe Trusso Sfrazzetto: implemented the NMR-DOSY characterization of CD. Giuseppe Nicotra: supervised TEM characterization of CD; wrote the draft article. Gianfranco Sfuncia: performed TEM characterization of CD; wrote the draft article. Elena Bruno: performed SEM characterization of CD; wrote the draft article, co-PI of the research project. Luca Lanzanò: performed FCS characterization of CD; wrote the draft article. Ignazio Barbagallo: performed cytotoxicity characterization of CD; wrote the draft article. Giovanni Li-Destri: performed AFM characterization of CD; supervised the activities, contributed to the data analysis, and wrote the paper. Nunzio Tuccitto: conceived the research, developed, and designed the methodology, supervised the activities, contributed to the data analysis, co-PI of the research project, and wrote the paper.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
